startScreen.startScreenSubtitle: A 58-year-old man presents with an acutely swollen, erythematous first metatarsophalangeal joint. Work through this case exploring DNA structure, replication, transcription, purine metabolism, and modern molecular techniques.

endScreens.endScreenSubtitle: You correctly linked purine metabolism to gout and demonstrated strong understanding of DNA replication, transcription, and molecular techniques.
You showed some understanding but missed key concepts. Review purine bases, DNA replication enzymes, and the relationship between uric acid and gout.
Revisit DNA structure (purines vs pyrimidines), replication machinery (helicase, DNA polymerase), transcription, and purine catabolism.

params.text:
Case Presentation

A 58-year-old man presents to the emergency department with severe pain and swelling of his right great toe. The joint is erythematous, warm, and exquisitely tender. He has a history of hypertension and chronic kidney disease.

Synovial fluid analysis reveals negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals — confirming gout. You recall that the final degradation product of purine metabolism in humans is uric acid, and its accumulation in joints causes this condition.

The two purine bases in DNA are adenine and guanine, both with a characteristic double-ring structure.

branchingQuestion.question: The patient's gout results from uric acid accumulation. During normal DNA replication, which enzyme unwinds the double helix, and in which direction does DNA polymerase synthesise the new strand?

alternatives.text: Helicase unwinds the helix; DNA polymerase synthesises in the 5' to 3' direction
RNA polymerase unwinds the helix; DNA polymerase synthesises in the 3' to 5' direction

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: RNA polymerase is involved in transcription, not replication. Helicase unwinds the DNA, and DNA polymerase always synthesises in the 5' to 3' direction.

alternatives.text: Topoisomerase unwinds the helix; DNA polymerase synthesises bidirectionally

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Topoisomerase relieves torsional strain but does not unwind the helix — that is helicase's role. DNA polymerase synthesises exclusively in the 5' to 3' direction.

params.text:
Further Investigation

Correct! Helicase unwinds DNA and polymerase synthesises 5'→3'.

The patient's serum uric acid is markedly elevated. You consider whether genetic factors might contribute. A colleague suggests genetic testing using PCR (polymerase chain reaction) to amplify specific DNA sequences, and mentions that CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing technology (derived from bacterial immune systems) could theoretically correct underlying metabolic enzyme defects.

You also recall that in transcription, the enzyme RNA polymerase reads the template DNA strand and synthesises a complementary mRNA molecule, which is then translated into the enzymes of purine metabolism.

branchingQuestion.question: To confirm a suspected enzyme deficiency contributing to hyperuricaemia, which molecular technique would you use to amplify the gene of interest from the patient's DNA?

alternatives.text: PCR (polymerase chain reaction) to amplify specific DNA sequences
CRISPR-Cas9 to cut and sequence the gene

feedback.title: Not quite
feedback.subtitle: CRISPR-Cas9 is a genome editing tool, not a diagnostic amplification technique. PCR is the standard method for amplifying specific DNA sequences for diagnosis.

alternatives.text: Reverse transcriptase to convert the gene to RNA for analysis

feedback.title: Incorrect
feedback.subtitle: Reverse transcriptase converts RNA to DNA (used by retroviruses and in RT-PCR). For amplifying a known DNA sequence, standard PCR using DNA polymerase is the correct choice.

params.text:
Clinical Synthesis

Excellent! You have correctly integrated molecular biology with clinical practice:

  • Purines: Adenine and guanine (double-ring); catabolised to uric acid → gout when accumulated
  • Replication: Helicase unwinds DNA; DNA polymerase synthesises 5'→3'
  • Transcription: RNA polymerase reads template DNA → produces mRNA
  • PCR: Amplifies specific DNA sequences for diagnostic purposes
  • CRISPR-Cas9: Genome editing derived from bacterial immune systems

This case demonstrates how fundamental molecular biology directly underlies clinical conditions like gout.
